system.description: A Castillian fencing style that forgoes the off-hand: the Duelist tucks it behind his back and presents his side, narrowing the targets an opponent can strike. Aldana combines fencing with dance, the Duelist silently keeping time to an unheard song so his movements stay unpredictable. Requires a fencing sword in one hand and nothing else.
system.bonus: Aldana Ruse. When you wield a fencing sword in one hand and nothing else, you can perform a special Maneuver called the Aldana Ruse: your target takes additional Wounds equal to your Ranks in Panache the next time he takes Wounds this Round. You can only perform Aldana Ruse once per Round.

system.description: A practical Vodacce style created by the famous courtesan Veronica Ambrogia. It teaches the sword in the left hand and the main gauche in the right, and prizes results over elegance — "it's the winner who tells the tale." Requires a dagger in the right hand and a fencing sword (rapier, cutlass) in the left.
system.bonus: Veronica's Guile. When wielding a dagger in your right hand and a fencing sword in your left, when you build a Risk Pool for Weaponry you may use either your Finesse or your Wits. If you spend a Hero Point, you may use both.

system.description: A fast, ungentlemanly knife-fighting style forged in the streets of Montaigne, built for Duelists who care more about results than impressions. Once the reach of a longer weapon is beaten, the knife-fighter opens a flurry that is nearly impossible to stop. Requires a dagger or knife in one hand with nothing in the other, or a dagger or knife in each hand.
system.bonus: Boucher Step. When wielding a dagger or knife in one hand with nothing in the other — or a dagger or knife in each hand — you can perform two Maneuvers back to back, spending Raises as normal for each, before your opponent can react. All other rules for Maneuvers still apply (you cannot perform the same Maneuver twice in a row). You can only use Boucher Step once per Round.

system.description: The bodyguard's art, founded by Donovan Durant — the most famous bodyguard in Théah — who, after his first ward died, swore never to let it happen again and kept that oath. His Academy in Carleon has the most rigid entry requirements of them all. Requires a heavy weapon (longsword, mace, etc.) in one hand and nothing in the other.
system.bonus: Donovan's Bulwark. When you wield a heavy weapon in one hand and nothing in the other, your Parry Maneuver is replaced by Donovan's Bulwark, preventing Wounds dealt to you or an ally within your reach equal to your Ranks in Weaponry. When you perform it you may also spend a Hero Point to lock weapons with your opponent: both of you lose all remaining Raises and can take no more Actions this Round.

system.description: A versatile Eisen style built around the two-handed zweihander by the mercenary Kristoff Drexel, leader of the Blood Spirits, who needed a way of fighting flexible enough for any situation. Popular among elite mercenaries. Requires a heavy weapon wielded in two hands.
system.bonus:
Drexel Stances. When you wield a heavy weapon in two hands, choose a Stance at the beginning of each Round (change mid-Round for 1 Raise):

  • Bittner Stance (Forward): Maneuvers that prevent Wounds prevent additional Wounds equal to your Resolve, but you cannot perform Slash (you can still spend Raises to deal Wounds as a non-Duelist).
  • Metzger Stance (Back): Take all Actions as if you had additional Raises equal to your Wits (these are only for taking Actions and cannot be spent), but you cannot perform Parry.
  • Gerbeck Stance (High): Maneuvers that deal Wounds deal 1 additional Wound, but you take all Actions as if you had 1 fewer Raise (minimum 1).

system.description: A patient, defensive Eisen style using a broadsword with an off-hand panzerhand (iron glove). The Duelist deflects or grabs incoming attacks with the glove and waits for the opponent to grow impatient, then exploits the first mistake. Requires a heavy weapon in one hand and a panzerhand in the other.
system.bonus: Iron Reply. When you wield a heavy weapon in one hand and a panzerhand in the other, your Riposte Maneuver is replaced by the Iron Reply, preventing Wounds equal to your Ranks in Resolve + your Ranks in Weaponry and dealing Wounds equal to your Ranks in Brawn + your Ranks in Weaponry. You can only perform Iron Reply once per Round.

system.description: An ancient, extremely aggressive Vesten style, formalized around two decades ago by the undefeated Leegstra Eldsdóttir at her Academy in Thorshofn. It teaches a weapon in each hand and encourages an almost reckless offense, burying opponents under a constant rain of blows. Requires a heavy weapon in each hand (typically axes or hammers; heavy-bladed broadswords are not uncommon).
system.bonus: Leegstra's Crash. When you wield a heavy weapon in each hand, you may perform a special Maneuver called Leegstra's Crash, dealing Wounds equal to your Ranks in Weaponry. You can only perform Leegstra's Crash once per Round.

system.description: A Vodacce style of misdirection and adaptability built around the whip, chosen by Vito Mantovani over a conspicuous sword. It uses the environment — snatching small items to hurl at an opponent, or laying a lash across a hand to make fighting on cost more than it's worth. Requires a whip.
system.bonus: Mantovani Flay. When you wield a whip, you gain a special Maneuver called Mantovani Flay. It deals one Wound, and you choose a Maneuver you know your opponent can perform (a basic Maneuver or a Style bonus you know they possess); your target cannot perform that Maneuver on their next Action. You can only perform Mantovani Flay once per Round.

system.description: The Ussuran sword-dance of Katya Mireli, a travelling circus dancer famous for a captivating routine with a curved sword in each hand. The Academy has no fixed campus — students must find Mireli or one of her students among travelling performers. Requires a heavy weapon (saber, cutlass, or even a hatchet) in one hand, or one in each hand.
system.bonus: Mireli's Revision. When you wield a heavy weapon in one hand, or one in each hand, you may perform a special Maneuver called Mireli's Revision, which functions identically to either the Bash or the Parry Maneuver — you choose the effect when you perform it.

system.description: A cerebral, defensive style from the chess grand master Mirek Sabat, who believed the strategies of the board applied to swordplay. It lets opponents attack relentlessly and exhaust themselves, then ends the bout decisively in a single master stroke. Requires a heavy weapon (typically a heavy curved saber) in one hand.
system.bonus: Sabat Gambit. When you wield a heavy weapon in one hand, your Lunge Maneuver is replaced by the Sabat Gambit, dealing Wounds equal to your Ranks in Weaponry + your Finesse + the number of Raises you spend.

system.description: A defensive style developed from bullfighting, using a rapier in one hand and a cloak in the other. The Duelist draws his opponent's attention to the cloak, then blindsides him with the rapier; the cloak and sword form a wall of cloth and steel. Requires a fencing sword (rapier, cutlass) in one hand, nothing in the other, and a long cloak.
system.bonus: Matador's Flourish. When you wield a fencing sword in one hand and nothing in the other, and are wearing a long cloak, you can perform a special Maneuver called the Matador's Flourish, preventing Wounds equal to your Ranks in Athletics. You can perform Matador's Flourish once per Round.

system.description: A Montaigne style using a fencing weapon in the primary hand and a main gauche in the off-hand. Technically defensive, it is built to demoralize: Valroux masters move the duel in a dizzying circle and hurl insults, forcing openings while their opponent loses patience. Requires a fencing sword in the right hand and a dagger in the left.
system.bonus: Valroux Press. When you wield a fencing sword in your right hand and a dagger in the left, you can perform a special Maneuver called Valroux Cross. It prevents Wounds equal to your Weaponry, and you choose a Maneuver you know your opponent can perform on her next Action; performing any other Maneuver costs your opponent an additional Raise. You can only perform Valroux Cross once per Round, immediately following the Maneuver that caused your Wounds.
